_meta.what_firm_does: Chime is a U.S. financial technology company (not a bank) that partners with banks to offer fee-free mobile banking — no overdraft fees, early direct deposit, secured credit-building products. Its stated mission is that everyone can achieve financial peace of mind and make progress.
_meta.jd_wants: A program manager to build the measurement frameworks, scorecards, and operational reporting that raise the performance bar across the Member Experience / dispute-ops organization, ideally with SQL/data fluency.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role at C: Performance effectiveness isn't just about scoring a team—from my experience, it's a continuous cycle of building the right measurement framework, reading the data honestly, and partnering across stakeholders to turn insight into accountability. This role is that instinct made into a job. At NextGen Healthcare I ran a manager-effectiveness initiative that translated 2,000+ feedback responses into scorecards and executive-ready reports that shaped strategy — the closest analog I have to building leadership review materials. And Chime's mission that everyone can achieve financial progress is the kind of work I'd be glad to keep the performance systems running behind. Diagnosing with data first, then recommending what to change, is genuinely how I work.
Tell us about a time you used data to dr: At Organization Development Network (ODN) Oregon, I worked with a team of consultants supporting an HR client that was sitting on a $362K leave-cost problem and a set of assumptions about why. Rather than hand them an answer, we partnered with their HR team to audit 300+ confidential leave cases, quantify the real costs, and refute the assumptions with evidence — then present executive-ready insights that led to actual policy redesigns and first-aid training. The result wasn't just a number; it was a client team that could now defend a decision with data instead of instinct. That work showed me how much it matters to move a conversation from assumptions to evidence — which is the heart of measuring performance honestly.
What is your experience with operational: My clearest reporting-and-metrics work is from NextGen Healthcare, where I analyzed 2,000+ employee-experience responses and translated them into scorecards and executive-ready reports that a non-technical C-suite could act on — turning messy feedback into decision-grade evidence. At InGenius Prep my data work is lighter and more operational: I track program enrollment data and work from an existing CRM dashboard rather than building reporting from scratch. I'll be candid about the tooling: my analysis has lived in spreadsheets, Excel, and SharePoint rather than SQL or BI platforms. I'm comfortable with data and pick up new tools quickly — I'd rather name that gap honestly than overstate it, and I'm genuinely eager to deepen there if the role needs it.
Anything else you'd like us to know?: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC. In practice that means I diagnose with data to find where performance actually breaks down, collaborate cross-functionally to design the fix, and keep tracking the metrics to make sure progress holds. I care as much about the people in the process as the process itself — which feels aligned with a company built around members' financial well-being. I'd love to bring that methodology to your Member Experience team.
